query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
answer:
**Headline Summary of the 2 sources**

* Medicaid policy documents provide guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims, including billing requirements and eligibility verification.
* Provider manual documents outline specific procedures for pharmacy services, including claim submission and recipient restriction programs.

**Policy Definition**

Medicaid dual eligible pharmacy claims are subject to specific billing requirements and eligibility verification. Providers must verify the eligibility of Medicaid members before submitting claims. Claim level identifiers are required for 340B-purchased drugs billed to NYRx or Medicaid Managed Care plans. Providers can access individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page.

[New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10 — pages [9]]: "providers must contact the individual plan" for Medicaid Managed Care Organization (MCO) billing guidance.
[New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11 — pages [6]]: "all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost."

**Provider Manual Definition**

Pharmacy providers of Medicaid services are required to verify the eligibility of Medicaid members using the Automated Response Unit (ARU) or ePACES web-based application. Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies.

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [10]]: "Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs..."
[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [40, 41]]: "Medicaid members who have been assigned to a designated pharmacy are required to receive all pharmacy services from the selected pharmacy provider."

**Comparison (similarities and differences)**

* Both sources require eligibility verification for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
* Provider manual documents provide more detailed procedures for claim submission and recipient restriction programs.
* Medicaid policy documents do not explicitly state procedures for claim submission, but rather refer providers to contact individual plans.

**Evidence (quoted)**

[New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10 — pages [9]]: "providers must contact the individual plan" for Medicaid Managed Care Organization (MCO) billing guidance.
[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [40, 41]]: "Medicaid members who have been assigned to a designated pharmacy are required to receive all pharmacy services from the selected pharmacy provider."

**Caveats**

Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for Provider Manual.

query: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
answer:
**Headline Summary of the 2 sources**

* Medicaid policy documents provide guidance on primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims.
* Provider manual documents outline procedures for handling third-party liability and recipient restriction programs.

**Policy Definition**

Medicaid dual eligible pharmacy claims require responsible third parties to pay as primary payors before submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is no responsible third party, the provider must pursue available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require providers to use all available resources before considering payment.

[New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11 — pages [7]]: "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first."
[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [17]]: "Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program."

**Provider Manual Definition**

Primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims is determined by the responsible third party. If a member has an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing NYRx as primary payor.

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [40, 41]]: "Medicaid members who have been assigned to a designated pharmacy are required to receive all pharmacy services from the selected pharmacy provider. All claims from other pharmacies will be denied."
[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [17]]: "NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9."

**Comparison (similarities and differences)**

* Both sources require responsible third parties to pay as primary payors before submitting a claim to Medicaid.
* Provider manual documents provide more detailed information on handling Workers’ Compensation cases and recipient restriction programs.

**Evidence (quoted)**

[New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11 — pages [7]]: "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first."
[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [17]]: "Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program."

**Caveats (if any)**

Insufficient evidence is found in the Provider Manual regarding the specific requirements for handling third-party liability and primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims.

query: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?
answer:
**Headline Summary of the 2 sources**

* Medicaid policy documents provide guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members.
* Provider manual documents outline procedures for handling co-payments and medical supplies.

**Policy Definition**

Medicaid regulations do not explicitly define co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members. However, [New York State Medicaid Update October 2024 Volume 40 Number 11] -[Chunk ID: mu_no11_oct24_pr.pdf::0031]-[pages: [12]] states that services provided to dual-eligible members are not eligible for incentive payments.

[Document Title: Children�s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children�s Home and Community Based Services Manual] -[Chunk ID: hcbs_manual (1).pdf::0044]-[pages: [45]] mentions that non-risk payment claims must include the same fields as in all other claims, but does not explicitly address co-payment responsibility.

**Provider Manual Definition**

The provider manual defines co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members. According to [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0056]-[pages: [39, 40]], pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to pay the co-payment.

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0012]-[pages: [10]] also states that pharmacies must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies.

**Comparison (similarities and differences)**

* Both sources acknowledge the importance of co-payments for dual-eligible members.
* The provider manual provides more detailed guidance on handling co-payments and medical supplies.

**Evidence (quoted)**

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0056]-[pages: [39, 40]]: "Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to pay the co-payment."
[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0012]-[pages: [10]]: "Pharmacies must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies."

**Caveats (if any)**

Insufficient evidence is found in Medicaid policy documents regarding co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members.

query: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
answer:
**Headline Summary of the 2 sources**

* Medicaid policy documents outline pharmacy responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing.
* Provider manual documents provide guidance on record-keeping requirements and handling of prescriptions.

**Policy Definition**

Pharmacies are responsible for using professional judgment in filling prescriptions without regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit and the assigned PCP is unavailable. [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1] -[Chunk ID: mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0015]-[pages: [7]].

Pharmacies must reverse all NYS Medicaid claims for drugs that were not deliverable within 60 days. [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10] -[Chunk ID: mu_no10_may23_pr.pdf::0030]-[pages: [12]].

**Provider Manual Definition**

Pharmacies must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of prescription drugs. [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0012]-[pages: [10]].

Pharmacies must keep on file the original prescription or fiscal order for which Medicaid payment is claimed, and these records must be kept for six years from the date the service is provided or billed. [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0035]-[pages: [26, 27]].

**Comparison (similarities and differences)**

query: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?
answer:
**Headline Summary of the 2 sources**

* Medicaid policy documents provide reimbursement guidelines for 340B drugs.
* Provider manual defines pharmacy reimbursement policies for prescription drugs, including 340B drugs.

**Policy Definition**
Medicaid reimburses 340B drugs at acquisition cost, inclusive of all discounts. [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11] -[Chunk ID: mu_no11_jun23_pr.pdf::0013]-[pages: [7]] 
When submitting a 340B drug claim, if the ingredient cost exceeds the ceiling price, the claim will be denied with an MEVS Rx Denial code "708: Exceeds NY Allowed Maximum". [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11] -[Chunk ID: mu_no11_jun23_pr.pdf::0014]-[pages: [7]] 
340B pharmacy drug claims must be submitted at the correct ingredient cost, which is the lowest price charged to the public after all applicable discounts. [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0053]-[pages: [36]] 

**Provider Manual Definition**
Pharmacy reimbursement for prescription drugs under the New York State Medicaid Program is established in law, and 340B drugs are reimbursed at the lower of NADAC, FUL, SMAC or U&C. [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0049]-[pages: [31]] 
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts for 340B drugs, and manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0062]-[pages: [43]] 

**Comparison (similarities and differences)**
Both policy and provider manual documents provide reimbursement guidelines for 340B drugs.

**Evidence (quoted)**

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11] -[Chunk ID: mu_no11_jun23_pr.pdf::0014]-[pages: [7]]

**Policy Definition**

query: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
answer:
**Headline Summary of the 2 sources**

* Medicaid policy documents provide billing requirements for home infusion drugs and supplies.
* Provider manual defines pharmacy reimbursement policies for home infusion treatments.

**Policy Definition**
Home infusion drugs must be billed as a pharmacy benefit. Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program and maximize Medicare benefits prior to billing Medicaid. [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10] -[Chunk ID: mu_no10_aug21_pr.pdf::0034]-[pages: [14, 15]] 
Pharmacies must also bill the administration fee for home infusion services and, when applicable, bill the acquisition cost of the vaccine using the appropriate procedure codes. [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2022 Volume 38 Number 10] -[Chunk ID: mu_no10_sep22_pr.pdf::0036]-[pages: [3]] 
No-Pay RAPs must be submitted within five calendar days prior to billing for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients. [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10] -[Chunk ID: mu_no10_aug21_pr.pdf::0034]-[pages: [14, 15]] 
Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will continue to access immunization services through their health plans. [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10] -[Chunk ID: mu_no10_aug21_pr.pdf::0019]-[pages: [9]] 

**Provider Manual Definition**
Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0027]-[pages: [18, 19]] 
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program and maximize Medicare benefits prior to billing Medicaid. [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0026]-[pages: [17, 18]] 
Pharmacists administering mental health or substance abuse treatment injections at the pharmacy must confirm the initial injection has been provided in the practitioner’s office. [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0022]-[pages: [15]] 

**Comparison (similarities and differences)**
Both policy and provider manual documents provide billing requirements for home infusion drugs and supplies.

**Evidence (quoted)**
Pharmacies must bill the administration fee for home infusion services and, when applicable, bill the acquisition cost of the vaccine using the appropriate procedure codes. No-Pay RAPs must be submitted within five calendar days prior to billing for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients. [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10] -[Chunk ID: mu_no10_aug21_pr.pdf::0034]-[pages: [14, 15]]
